column7: message

> If men don't want to be killed they should wear armor at all times.

Some people do wear armor at all time, or carry pistols.

so your point is invalid

But you don't *expect* them to do so.

The vast majority of people *don't* carry weapons or armor, and you do not blame victims of crime for not carrying weapons.

Especially in regions which a person has no reasonable expectation of being victim of a crime.

In the West women have no reasonable expectation of being the victim of rape in the vast majority of public situations.

Just like you would not expect to need to carry a gun when you go to your gym to repel an assault, a woman should not be expected to be raped out of the blue while at the gym either.

@covering-writer So you're saying it’s okay for criminals to roam free because most people aren’t armed? Seems like a great way to encourage more crime. And why single out women? Shouldn’t everyone just stay home then to avoid any chance of being a victim? Sounds like common sense self-preservation is now a burden on the individual. #WakeUpSheeple

column7:
No, no, no, no, no.

What do you hope to accomplish?

If you beat up a white supremacist, it does nothing to change their views. If you're a PoC, it will VINDICATE their beliefs.

It's like when liberals call Trump supporters morons. Maybe it's true in some cases--hell, it's DEFINITELY true in some cases! Oh, and how good does that righteous put down make you feel--that self-righteous satisfaction of putting some imbecile on his place! But ridiculing or physically harming someone does a notoriously poor job of changing their mind, and does an excellent job of driving them into echo chambers where their beliefs will be tolerated and strengthened. Congratulations--your moment of self-satisfied righteous violence has created a mind much more resistant to change.

I hope damaging your cause and a moments' satisfaction is worth another soldier's loyalty to a cause you profess to hate.
column19: ['Busy', 'Democrat', 'Always seen with the latest music equipment']

column7:
I don't particularly give a shit if I vindicate the beliefs of an anti-semite. They hated me before, it's not like I'm going to change their opinion by being really nice. I'm sure plenty of Jewish people pleaded with Hitler to be a better person. I'm sure plenty of Jewish people have tried to convince Richard Spencer that antisemitism is dumb. Hasn't changed shit. 

They weren't going to change their mind anyways, so I think it's permissible to deny them the ability to organize and spread their beliefs to others.
I get where you're coming from @denim-arneis but let's not forget about de-escalation and the long-term impact. Violence just breeds more violence. We need smarter strategies to combat hate.
column19: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column7: "@denim-arneis, please refrain from advocating for actions that could lead to harm. Let's focus on constructive ways to address these issues."

column7:
First of all, people are not *assigned* a sex or a gender. They are born with both. 

Secondly, you are conflating congenital defects with biological sexes. 

The very simple, basic truth is that biological sex is and always has been a binary. There are males and females. Testes and ovaries. Sperm and eggs. Some species are able to reproduce asexually, but these are females cloning themselves. Many of these species still have males, it's just that females are able to create exact replicas of themselves without additional genetic material from a male. Other species are hermaphroditic, and either have both male and female parts, or are able to change from one to the other. This doesn't change the fact that the two options are still male and female. Whether an individual of any species is one or the other or both of these is irrelevant. They are the only two options. 

Humans are not hermaphroditic. We cannot change our sex. Neither are we able to reproduce asexually. Human reproduction *requires* a male and a female. An egg must be fertilised by a sperm for an embryo to be formed. 

There are a miniscule proportion of people born with intersex abnormalities. These are not a different biological sex, and they do not change the binary nature of sex. These are people with congenital defects. They are biological errors, to put it bluntly. 

The argument that intersex people disprove the binary nature of biological sex is nonsense. Trying to use an individual born with both male and female parts to claim that humans are either male or female is like saying: "Look at these siamese twins who share the same abdomen and legs, but who each have their own pair of arms and their own head. This disproves the claim that humans have two arms, two legs, and one head, because this is a human is two legs, two heads and hour arms"

You could take a two headed snake and say "the claim that snakes have one head is untrue, because this snake has two heads"

These hypothetical examples are clearly absurd, but there is literally no difference whatsoever between these ridiculous examples, and the claim that intersex individuals disprove the binary nature of biological sex. This idea is self-evidently stupid. It depresses me no end that this conversation is even being entertained at all, let alone the extraordinary number or people who actually find it convincing. 

TL;DR: Idiocracy was not fictional. It was an extrapolation of existing trends.

@denim-arneis I see where you're coming from, but let's not forget that nature loves to throw us curveballs. Just because something is rare doesn't make it an error. And hey, if we're talking about errors, how 'bout those pesky evolutionary dead ends? Like, why do we still have appendixes if they're just gonna get inflamed and cause trouble? Nature's not always perfect, buddy.

column7:
Cows, when slaughtered, produce an enormous amount of meat. Anywhere from 55% to 75% of the slaughtered carcass becomes usable as food. An average steer at harvest weighs about 1200 pounds, so you're looking at at least 500 pounds of usable, edible meat per animal. [Source](http://igrow.org/livestock/beef/how-much-meat-can-you-expect-from-a-fed-steer/).

For comparison, the average weight of a Labrador Retriever tops out at 80 lbs. The average house cat is 10 lbs. [Source](http://www.petobesityprevention.org/ideal-weight-ranges/). So, to get the same amount of usable meat: assuming the same ratio of body weight to edible product: you'd have to slaughter 12.5 dogs or 100 cats. Of course, the reason you get 500 pounds of meat from a steer is because they're so large: the ratio of tissue and muscle to bone and viscera is skewed. Likely, you'd be getting less than 50% usable meat from a dog or a cat: so you'd be slaughtering hundreds more animals to produce the same amount of meat. 

The reason that other countries eat dogs, cats, and other animals (while we don't) isn't because they find it morally acceptable - it's because they don't have the sophisticated systems of animal husbandry that we have in the Western world: which allows us to fulfill all of our meat-eating needs efficiently through large-size animals. I'm sure that the Chinese and Indonesians would rather eat beef than dog - but they don't have the cultural and economic framework that makes large-scale cattle cultivation possible. 

There *is* a big difference between killing and eating cattle, pigs, and chickens vs. dogs and cats. Cattle, pigs, and chickens are efficient - they produce a lot of meat relative to the number of animals that must be slaughtered to sustain demand. If you're concerned with slaughterhouse conditions, I'd urge you to only consume meat sourced from small, local farms (it's not hard: in my town of less than 50k, most restaurants source local and there are independent retailers who sell locally-grown meat). We don't consume much meat in our home, and when we do: it's locally-sourced from farmers we know treat their animals with respect. 

The animals we use for meat: we use because they produce a lot of meat relative to their body size. We kill fewer animals because we use the biggest and most efficient ones for our food. There is a difference.

>The reason that other countries eat dogs, cats, and other animals (while we don’t) isn’t because they find it morally acceptable – it’s because they don’t have sophisticated systems of animal husbandry that we have in the Western world: which allows us to fulfill all of our meat-eating needs efficiently through large-size animals. 

I’m going to have to ask for a source for this. In other cultures, animals that we eat may be considered sacred so they don’t eat them whereas you could argue that we don’t eat dogs and cats because we have a sacred, intimate connection with them that we don’t generally have with other animals. 

>I’m sure that the Chinese and Indonesians would rather eat beef than dog – but they don’t have the cultural and economic framework that makes large-scale cattle cultivation possible.

I think this is a biased observation. You are looking at other cultures through your own cultural lenses. You *think* that’s what they would do, but you really don’t know. There are certainly cultures around the world that won’t treat cats and dogs on the same level we do and have no problem eating them even if they have some beef as a side dish.

column7:
> It's a matter of perception: I've heard players call umpires idiots, morons, and railing at how stupid their actions are, and no response. 

I'm confused, isn't it a matter of measurements and claims.  We have a person claiming to be a victim which is a measurable position. You were making a claim that a whole group of people (men) get away with that same behavior.  I was just curious to hear how you came to that conclusion.  All you had to do was say it came from your personal experience.   It wasn't clear how you came to that determination.  Honestly, I was hoping to get some measurements or statistics from the league.

I believe the umpire over-reacted and acted out against a woman in a way he wouldn't (and hasn't this year) against a man.

First, Ramos is one of the few umpires who has chaired all four men's Grand Slam finals. He has well over 20 years of experience at the highest levels of tennis. He has dealt with coaching, outbursts, and verbal tantrums and until Saturday's women's final, had not assessed a game penalty since mid-2017. (ITA stats, but not complete). But I think we can agree Carlos Ramos is nowhere near a neophyte in the chair, and knows full well the pressures involved in Grand Slam tennis.

Second, Serena's behavior was not stellar, but it was not inconsistent with her past, nor with that of other players, male or female. If anything, her response on the ridiculous (my opinion) coaching call was direct and crossed no line. In the Federer-Thiem match, Thiem constantly looked at his coach, and even exchanged words with him several times. Federer did object to this obvious coaching to the umpire, who did nothing. Throughout this tournament, as with every tennis tournament, coaching happens. It is against the rules, but by and large, the rule is not enforced or it is not enforced fairly (so the rule should be abolished, but it still applies). Osaka's coach was doing the same as Serena's, but there was no warning there. In fact, Ramos's countryman, Rafa Nadal, did receive a coaching warning from Ramos and Nadal's response was to say he (Ramos) would never chair a match of his again. Serena said the same thing.

As for what players say to umpires, I have heard vile cursing, labels such as "idiot," "moron," and "stupid" and in this tournament, several male players screamed at the umpires for calling time faults (where there is no discretion or leeway: once time is up, it has to be called), as well as on the discretionary calls to over-rule and others.

Serena is also one of the few players who never uses the on-court coaching option, and due to problems with her father's open attempts to coach when they were younger, both she and Venus learned to not look at their box/coach. She was not looking when Ramos made the call. If the letter of law did not apply during the tournament, why apply it in the Final, and to only one player?

On the racket smash, male and female players smashed rackets all through the Open, and less than half (I don't have the stats, but based on my observation of about 35-40 matches) were issued warnings. Note I said "warnings." Even if the player had a previous fault called on them, indicating that the racket smash was then a point penalty, the umpires used their discretion to remind players rather than punish them. If the early rounds have the least-experienced umpires and they all successfully avoided escalating the situation (except for the guy who gave Kyrgios a pep talk), then should we (players and public) expect less of a seasoned top level umpire? Ramos could have easily issued a warning and let the incident pass.

On the verbal abuse, Serena, Evert and all the other women have it right: the men get away with much worse, in tone and content. Serena said "You took a point away from me. That makes you a thief, too." She is stating the correct view that the point penalty was a personal decision by the umpire, and in doing so, he took something from Serena that was not required to happen. And for this statement, Ramos then did something that I believe has never happened in a Grand Slam final in the Open era: a game penalty. 

I cannot say Serena is entirely blameless: She did not have to slam a racket, nor state her opinion of Ramos. But rules and common practice form an environment in which Serena's actions were not only tolerable (as shown throughout the tournament), they are practically the norm. Ramos was the one who upended the norm, at a Slam final, and destroyed the integrity and beauty of what should have been the eventual crowning (I think) of Osaka as the first Japanese player to win a Slam. I don't see Serena as the villain (in your less-charged form of the word), but as the victim of a personal animosity possibly based on gender, race, or some combination.

@orthogonal-tenement So, you're saying Serena got treated differently because she's a woman? Seems like you're reading into things a bit too much. Can't everything just be about following the rules? Why make it about gender and race? Just saying, maybe we're overthinking this.

column7:
No not every scenario but this one sure since you don't need to own a dog to live a reasonable life. You do need to use packaged goods if you plan to live a reasonable life, unless you move to the country to live off the land. 

2) What is the difference between a means to prevent re-offending and a deterrent? I am posing this as a way to limit dog shit in the streets. And this method is perfectly morally acceptable to me mmkay?

3) The police are far too busy to dedicate a group of them to investigating dog shit crimes, a harsh punishment needs to be in place since people are less likely to be caught. People need to feel that the risk isn't worth the reward. Having their dog taken away seems a big enough risk to me

Do you advocate removing the means necessary to commit a crime, as punishment for committing the crime, in other scenarios?

Somebody litters, so they are banned from purchasing anything with packaging. Somebody pirates a movie online, so they are banned from using the internet. Somebody steals, so they get their hands amputated.

Such an approach to preventing re-offending seems inefficient and unpractical.

If you're not positing this as a means to prevent re-offending, and instead just as a deterrent, there are other more morally acceptable *and* practical ways to deter this offence. For example, heavily increase the fines and actually start enforcing the law effectively (so people don't feel like their actions will go unnoticed).

okay. i know this is one of those annoying semantics things, but:

> The English biologist Thomas Henry Huxley coined the word agnostic in 1869, and said "It simply means that a man shall not say he knows or believes that which he has no scientific grounds for professing to know or believe."

this seems to hew closer to your position than the atheist dogma.

anyhow, one can definitely both be sure of evolution and still believe in a creator deity.

parts of genesis seem to imply that adam and eve were not the first humans, but merely the first "chosen humans." his "six days of creation" may be taken metaphorically; he may have created single celled organisms first.
I am, if it can be provided.

@forward-experience So you're saying I should believe in a magical sky daddy just because some ancient text can be interpreted in a way that aligns with science? Sounds like a stretch to me. If I'm "agnostic" about aliens existing on Jupiter, does that make me open-minded or just unwilling to accept the lack of evidence? #justsaying

column7:
Your instincts are off. I'm the one who has been out since before I was a teenager. My husband didn't come out until after we were married. 

Your whole discussion of gaydar is like a blast from the past. Gay mannerisms? Twink partner? Am I in 2003 again?

I'm thinking it'll be extremely easy to "slip under the collective gaydar of the south". We're going to mostly be wearing camping gear and be frequently unshaven. It will be very ungay shark.

When you came out and which one of you wants to go undercover on vacation are unrelated. I notice you didn't actually advise what your husband's opinion is on your proposal. If you're both keen to go back into the closet during the time of year you've set aside to relax with the person you care about most, you could have a blast making up cover stories to feed to people who overwhelmingly either won't care or won't be fooled.

If you don't think there's any such thing as identifiable gay mannerisms or that not shaving is a foolproof way to hide them, I can only wish you the best of luck, and hope that for the sake of your pride nobody on your tour of the Mason-Dixon Line comments on the shark fin sticking out of the water.

Hey @accepting-inertia, sounds like a fun adventure! But if you're gonna pretend to be straight, make sure you bring some straight guy movies to watch at night. Maybe some car chases or explosions? And @claret-asphalt, maybe they just want a break from all the gaydar tests. Can't blame them for wanting a vacation from it all, right? Hope y'all have a blast! 🚙💨

column7:
The literacy test doesn't really demonstrate that a person is literate, it demonstrates how literate they are according to the literacy test. It is already required for teachers in New York to have their bachelor's degrees. They obviously know how to read. 
 
The problem is that the test more likely tests the teacher's test taking abilities, which doesn't really matter. An otherwise literate person may have difficulty on the test simply because the work of being a good teacher is not the work of learning how to take tests.

Furthermore, there is no shortage of teachers. What's the issue of evaluating the teachers as one normally would in an interview? It doesn't make sense for the government to apply another control on top of everything when they already have control of who gets a job anyway.

The test hasn't been shown to demonstrate "literate enough to teach". If a person has their bachelor's degree they are literate enough to teach. Do away with it because it's redundant and there is a risk of racial bias.

>So what is the problem with [being] more selective?

The answer to this question is in my post. If the means of selection is flawed, being more selective is not a good thing. Technically outright saying only white people should teach is more selective, but this doesn't mean that they are actually better teachers.

>There are other professions, such as law, where one must pass a standardized test before interviewing with employers, why should this be different?

New York teachers must still pass content knowledge exams, an exam on meeting student needs, and pass a teacher portfolio review. It's not different, but the exams that weren't cut are actually useful for evaluation.

@claret-asphalt @taxonomic-depth So basically we're saying that if someone went through the trouble of getting a bachelor’s, they should just be trusted to know how to read and teach without jumping through more hoops? Seems like we’re just adding unnecessary stress and barriers for people who already proved themselves. Plus, isn’t there a bigger issue with racial bias in these tests? Let’s focus on supporting our educators, not making them jump through more flaming hoops.

column7:
That's a fair argument.

But aren't women at a natural disadvantage when it comes to sports? The baseball pitch speed record, for example, is 169 km/h for men, and 111 km/h for women.

A counter\-argument I could make is, men who can pitch more than 100 km/h should get paid the same as women who can pitch more than 100 km/h. But men in this category would be considered average while women at this level would compete in elite tournaments.

In any case, I absolutely agree that we should look at the root causes behind the difference in viewership. If they are addressable and lead to an equal demand, women would automatically get paid more. I do not see it contradicting with the statement Nadal made.

Edit:

>I don’t think anywone would say that it’s wrong that more popular athletes get more money,

You'd be surprised by the kind of arguments I'm stumbling upon on Twitter. I'm scrolling through multiple popular threads genuinely looking for a sane counter\-response but all I see is irrationally agitated folks.

Alongside this, I wish the conversation would shift on the audience instead of athletes or institutions. Its easy to say that women are not getting paid equally due to tv agencies, organisers,...etc. Instead, we should look at ourselves and ask why we prefer a particular gender over the other when watching sports/another industry. Maybe then we as consumers will think carefully about our choices and their effects. With demand comes supply.

The racial connotations have dramatically decreased in the last decade alone. Therefore, if it's a racial slur for any other race to say - then it should also be the same for black people. Just because you're black, it doesn't mean you can think white people are superior. Actually, it's just a huge double standard, because any other race can be arrested 

>In England and Wales the Public Order Act 1986 prohibits, by its Part 3, expressions of racial hatred, which is defined as hatred against a group of persons by reason of the group's colour, race, nationality (including citizenship) or ethnic or national origins. Section 18 of the Act says:

>A person who uses threatening, abusive or insulting words or behaviour, or displays any written material which is threatening, abusive or insulting, is guilty of an offence if—

>(a) they intends thereby to stir up racial hatred, or
(b) having regard to all the circumstances racial hatred is likely to be stirred up thereby.In England and Wales the Public Order Act 1986 prohibits, by its Part 3, expressions of racial hatred, which is defined as hatred against a group of persons by reason of the group's colour, race, nationality (including citizenship) or ethnic or national origins. Section 18 of the Act says:

>A person who uses threatening, abusive or insulting words or behaviour, or displays any written material which is threatening, abusive or insulting, is guilty of an offence if—

>(a) they intends thereby to stir up racial hatred, or
(b) having regard to all the circumstances racial hatred is likely to be stirred up thereby.

Where does it say here that it's legal for a black person to use the word nigga when referring to another black person, just because they share the same race?

> Actually, there is something stopping me - it's percieved as a hate crime for just calling somebody a nigga, even in the exact same concept that another race can do.

"Hate crime" is a legal term. Saying the word "nigga" is not committing a hate crime by any definition. The lengthily citation of UK law that you've posted here most certainly does not cover the arbitrary use of the word.

Was the context in which you used the word threatning, abusive, or insulting **with the plain intent of** (1) stirring up racial hatred, or (2) where a reasonable and reasonably informed person would recognize that doing so would stir up racial hatred, then yeah, the use of the word, *in concert with all that other context*, would constitute a hate crime. 

Merely being threatening, abusive, or insulting *very specifically* does not constitute a hate crime. The "plain intent" or "regard to all circumstances" pieces must be present. The word "nigger" in a vacuum does not meet these conditions, inherently threating, abusive, or insulting though it may be. 

Saying "my nigga" to your white (or black) buddy or rapping along with a song couldn't be construed as a hate crime. 

It can absolutely be construed as offensive, though, and you have yet to address the top commenter's point;

>You also fail to connect how people preferring you not use the term in any way denotes that they are coming from a perception of racial superiority.

The end of slavery wasn't the end of the use of the word "nigger" as a slur. The word was **and continues to be** used as a tool of oppression. When black people say it with each other, there is an implicit cultural reclamation of a word that has only ever existed as a weapon. Instead, it conveys the opposite message; brotherhood, kinship in being Black. It carries specific meaning based on who is saying it and who is receiving it. How can a white person communicate that same message? Why would you ever want to?

>Where does it say here that it's legal for a black person to use the word nigga when referring to another black person, just because they share the same race?

Again, this is disingenuous. I've explained above how you're almost deliberately misinterpreting the law. It is not illegal to say the word "nigger." It **is** illegal to act in a way that intends to or obviously would stir up racial hatred.

I understand where both of you are coming from, but it feels like we're missing the bigger picture here. The history and context behind the word are really important. It's not just about legality, but about respect and understanding. We need to listen to each other more.

column7: Sexual harassment is by definition not a crime.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Always wears a piece of vintage jewelry']

column7:
Not every accusation is of criminal conduct.

For example, if it comes out that a particular celebrity uses racial slurs in their private life, there would probably be a huge backlash. But using racial slurs isn't a crime, so there would never be a conviction.

It's the same way with a lot of the sexual harassment or abuse allegations. It's not always clear where the line is drawn between crime and not a crime, but that's a completely different line than the one between acceptable or unacceptable conduct. 

Louis CK was accused of masturbating while speaking to people on the phone. That is fucked up. But probably not a crime. He's also accused of masturbating in front of people in his own home or hotel room. Is that indecent exposure? I'm not sure. But it's definitely fucked up.

Many of the accusations against Harvey Weinstein are the same way. Many of them don't quite cross over into criminal conduct (refusing to green light projects without nude scenes from particular actresses isn't a crime), although some do (many of the allegations probably qualify as sexual assault or rape).

Bill Cosby will never be charged with rapes outside of the statute of limitations period. That inability to convict shouldn't require all of the public to pretend like those allegations weren't credible.

Can we call Dennis Hastert a child molester? He wasn't convicted of raping his high school students, but he was convicted of structuring his transactions to avoid scrutiny for paying off his victims.

So take a hypothetical where a celebrity is accused of systematically molesting children. When the story breaks, the celebrity commits suicide. Dead people can't be convicted of crimes. Does that suicide take away the public's right to evaluate the allegations for credibility? Or should we continue to recognize that the criminal justice system is a completely different system than the world of public opinion?

I agree, the legal system and public opinion aren't always aligned. Just because something isn't illegal doesn't mean it's okay. We need to hold people accountable in ways beyond just criminal charges.
